title: File & Directory Management
obj_title: Manage files and directories on a Linux system
goal: Practice core file and directory operations: navigating, creating, copying, searching, linking, and inspecting special device files with standard Linux utilities.
build: Create a lab tree, then copy, find, link, diff, and inspect files and /dev device nodes.

steps.title: Navigate and list
steps.code:
pwd
cd /tmp
mkdir -p lab08/{src,dst,archive}
cd lab08
ls -la
steps.explain: pwd shows the cwd; ls -la lists hidden files with full metadata.

steps.title: Create, copy, move, touch
steps.code:
touch src/file1.txt src/file2.log
echo "hello" > src/file1.txt
cp -a src/file1.txt dst/
mv src/file2.log archive/
stat dst/file1.txt
file dst/file1.txt
steps.explain: cp -a preserves attributes; stat shows inode details and file identifies content type.

steps.title: Find files by name, time, and size
steps.code:
dd if=/dev/zero of=src/big.bin bs=1M count=5
find /tmp/lab08 -name "*.txt"
find /tmp/lab08 -mtime -1
find /tmp/lab08 -size +1M
find /tmp/lab08 -name "*.bin" -exec ls -lh {} \;
steps.explain: find walks the tree with predicates; -exec runs a command per match.

steps.title: locate and lsof
steps.code:
apt update -y && apt install -y mlocate lsof
updatedb
locate file1.txt | head
sleep 300 &
lsof -p $! | head
steps.explain: locate queries a prebuilt DB; lsof lists open files held by a process.

steps.title: diff and sdiff
steps.code:
echo -e "alpha
bravo
charlie" > a.txt
echo -e "alpha
bravo2
charlie
delta" > b.txt
diff a.txt b.txt
sdiff a.txt b.txt
steps.explain: diff shows line differences; sdiff gives a side-by-side view with markers.

steps.title: Hard links vs symbolic links
steps.code:
echo "original" > target.txt
ln target.txt hardlink.txt
ln -s target.txt symlink.txt
ls -li target.txt hardlink.txt symlink.txt
rm target.txt
cat hardlink.txt
cat symlink.txt || echo "symlink broken"
steps.explain: Hard links share an inode and survive deletion; symlinks break when the target is gone.

steps.title: Device files in /dev
steps.code:
ls -l /dev/sda /dev/null /dev/tty1 2>/dev/null
echo "discarded" > /dev/null
head -c 16 /dev/urandom | xxd
steps.explain: The ls -l first column marks block (b) vs character (c) devices; /dev/null discards writes.

test: ls -li confirms hardlink shares the target's inode while the symlink breaks after the target is removed.

title: Local Account & Group Management
obj_title: Perform local account management in a Linux environment
goal: Create and manage local users and groups, set passwords and aging policies, change shells, and distinguish user, system, and service accounts.
build: Create users/groups, set passwords and aging, inspect identity databases, then remove the accounts.
concepts: UID>=1000 user vs UID<1000 system vs service (-r)

steps.title: Inspect account databases
steps.code:
head -5 /etc/passwd
head -5 /etc/shadow
head -5 /etc/group
ls /etc/skel -la
head -20 /etc/profile
steps.explain: passwd holds metadata, shadow holds hashed passwords, and skel templates new home dirs.

steps.title: Create users and groups
steps.code:
groupadd developers
useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G developers alice
adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" bob
usermod -aG developers bob
id alice
groups bob
steps.explain: useradd is low-level, adduser is the Debian wrapper; usermod -aG appends groups without dropping existing ones.

steps.title: Passwords and aging
steps.code:
echo "alice:Passw0rd!" | chpasswd
passwd -S alice
chage -l alice
chage -M 90 -W 7 alice
chage -l alice
steps.explain: chage sets aging policy: -M max age in days, -W the warning window.

steps.title: Change shell and identify accounts
steps.code:
chsh -s /bin/sh bob
getent passwd alice bob
whoami
id
sudo -u alice id
steps.explain: chsh updates the login shell; sudo -u shows the effective UID/GID when running as another user.

steps.title: User vs system vs service accounts
steps.code:
awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $3 < 65534 {print "USER  ", $1, $3}' /etc/passwd
awk -F: '$3 < 1000 {print "SYSTEM", $1, $3}' /etc/passwd | head
getent passwd www-data
useradd -r -s /usr/sbin/nologin svc_app
getent passwd svc_app
steps.explain: Users are UID>=1000, system accounts UID<1000, service accounts use -r plus nologin.

steps.title: Login history and active sessions
steps.code:
last | head
lastlog | head
who
w
steps.explain: last reads wtmp history, lastlog shows last login per user, w/who show current sessions.

steps.title: Remove users and groups
steps.code:
userdel -r bob
userdel -r svc_app
groupdel developers
getent passwd bob || echo "bob removed"
steps.explain: userdel -r removes the account plus home and mail spool; remove users before their primary group.

test: getent passwd bob returns nothing after userdel -r, confirming the account and home directory are gone.

title: Processes, Jobs & Scheduling
obj_title: Manage processes and jobs in a Linux environment
goal: Inspect processes, manipulate shell jobs, adjust priorities, send signals, and schedule tasks with at, cron, and anacron.
build: Examine processes via ps/proc/strace, control background jobs, renice, signal, and schedule jobs.
concepts: jobs: &, Ctrl+Z, bg, fg, nohup, disown

steps.title: Inspect running processes
steps.code:
ps auxf | head -20
pstree -p | head -20
apt update -y && apt install -y htop sysstat lsof strace at
pidstat 1 3
mpstat 1 2
steps.explain: ps auxf shows a process forest; pidstat/mpstat sample per-process and per-CPU stats.

steps.title: Explore /proc and lsof
steps.code:
sleep 600 &
SPID=$!
ls /proc/$SPID/
cat /proc/$SPID/status | head
cat /proc/$SPID/cmdline; echo
lsof -p $SPID | head
steps.explain: Each process has a /proc/<PID> dir; lsof -p lists all its open file descriptors.

steps.title: Process states and strace
steps.code:
ps -o pid,stat,cmd -p $SPID
strace -p $SPID -e trace=nanosleep -o /tmp/strace.out &
sleep 2
kill %2 2>/dev/null
head /tmp/strace.out
steps.explain: STAT codes show process state; strace -p attaches to a live PID to trace system calls.

steps.title: Background, foreground, jobs
steps.code:
sleep 300 &
sleep 400 &
jobs
fg %1
# Ctrl+Z to suspend, then:
bg %1
jobs
nohup sleep 500 > /tmp/nohup.out 2>&1 &
disown
jobs
steps.explain: & backgrounds, Ctrl+Z suspends, bg/fg resume, and nohup+disown detach a job to survive logout.

steps.title: Priority with nice and renice
steps.code:
nice -n 10 sleep 200 &
NPID=$!
ps -o pid,ni,cmd -p $NPID
renice -n 5 -p $NPID
ps -o pid,ni,cmd -p $NPID
steps.explain: Nice ranges -20 (highest) to 19 (lowest); only root can raise priority.

steps.title: Signals: kill, pkill, killall
steps.code:
sleep 1000 &
TPID=$!
kill -HUP $TPID 2>/dev/null; echo "HUP sent"
sleep 1000 &
kill -15 $!
sleep 1000 &
kill -9 $!
sleep 1000 & sleep 1000 &
pkill -f "sleep 1000"
killall sleep 2>/dev/null
steps.explain: -15 (TERM) asks graceful exit, -9 (KILL) forces it, -1 (HUP) reloads; pkill matches pattern, killall by name.

steps.title: Scheduling with at and cron
steps.code:
service atd start 2>/dev/null || systemctl start atd
echo "date > /tmp/at-fired.txt" | at now + 1 minute
atq
( crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo "* * * * * date >> /tmp/cron.log" ) | crontab -
crontab -l
ls /etc/anacrontab && head /etc/anacrontab
steps.explain: at schedules a one-shot job, cron runs recurring five-field jobs, anacron catches missed jobs.

test: atq lists the queued at job and crontab -l shows the recurring entry, confirming both schedulers accepted the tasks.

title: Software & Package Management
obj_title: Configure and manage software in a Linux environment
goal: Manage Debian packages with apt/dpkg, add a signed third-party repo, run dnf/rpm in a container, and use language and sandboxed package managers.
build: Install/remove apt packages, add a GPG-signed repo, demo dnf/rpm, then pip/npm/cargo and snap/flatpak.

steps.title: apt basics
steps.code:
apt update
apt install -y tree jq
apt-cache policy jq
dpkg -l | grep -E "tree|jq"
dpkg -L tree | head
steps.explain: apt manages packages/deps; dpkg -l lists installed packages and dpkg -L lists a package's files.

steps.title: Add a third-party repo with GPG key
steps.code:
apt install -y curl gnupg lsb-release
install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings
curl -fsSL https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/hashicorp.gpg
echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/hashicorp.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
apt update
apt-cache policy terraform | head
steps.explain: Modern Debian/Ubuntu uses per-repo signed-by keyrings instead of deprecated apt-key.

steps.title: Remove packages
steps.code:
apt remove -y tree
apt purge -y jq
apt autoremove -y
steps.explain: remove keeps config, purge deletes it too, autoremove cleans orphaned dependencies.

steps.title: RPM world inside a container
steps.code:
apt install -y docker.io
systemctl start docker
docker run --rm rockylinux:9 bash -c "dnf -y install nano > /dev/null && rpm -q nano && rpm -V nano || true && rpm -qa | head"
steps.explain: dnf is the Red Hat package manager; rpm -q queries install status and rpm -V verifies integrity.

steps.title: Language-specific package managers
steps.code:
apt install -y python3-pip nodejs npm cargo
pip3 install --user --break-system-packages requests
python3 -c "import requests; print(requests.__version__)"
npm install -g cowsay
cowsay "Linux+"
cargo --version
steps.explain: pip installs Python libs, npm -g installs Node CLIs globally, cargo is Rust's build/package tool.

steps.title: update-alternatives
steps.code:
update-alternatives --display editor
update-alternatives --list editor
steps.explain: update-alternatives manages symlinks for commands with multiple providers (editor, java, etc.).

steps.title: Sandboxed package formats
steps.code:
which snap && snap list 2>/dev/null || echo "snap not preinstalled on this image"
apt install -y flatpak
flatpak --version
flatpak remotes
steps.explain: Snap and Flatpak ship apps with bundled dependencies in confined sandboxes.

test: apt-cache policy terraform shows a candidate from the signed HashiCorp repo, confirming the GPG-verified source is trusted.

title: Service Management with systemd
goal: Inspect and control systemd units, author custom service/timer/mount units, analyze boot performance, and configure host/time/DNS via systemd helpers.
build: Manage nginx, write a service + timer + mount unit, analyze boot time, and set hostname/timezone/DNS.

steps.title: List and inspect units
steps.code:
systemctl list-units --type=service | head -20
systemctl list-unit-files --type=service | head -20
systemctl --failed
steps.explain: list-units shows loaded units; list-unit-files shows all installed units and their enabled state.

steps.title: Control nginx
steps.code:
apt update -y && apt install -y nginx
systemctl status nginx --no-pager
systemctl stop nginx; systemctl start nginx; systemctl restart nginx; systemctl reload nginx
systemctl disable nginx; systemctl enable nginx
systemctl mask nginx && systemctl start nginx 2>&1 | head
systemctl unmask nginx
steps.explain: enable/disable toggles autostart, reload re-reads config live, and mask makes a unit unstartable.

steps.title: Author a custom service
steps.code:
cat > /usr/local/bin/lab-hello.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "lab-hello fired at $(date)" >> /var/log/lab-hello.log
EOF
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/lab-hello.sh
cat > /etc/systemd/system/lab-hello.service <<'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Lab Hello one-shot service
[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/lab-hello.sh
EOF
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl start lab-hello.service
cat /var/log/lab-hello.log
steps.explain: daemon-reload re-parses unit files; Type=oneshot suits short scripts that exit when done.

steps.title: Add a timer
steps.code:
cat > /etc/systemd/system/lab-hello.timer <<'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Run lab-hello every minute
[Timer]
OnBootSec=30s
OnUnitActiveSec=1min
Unit=lab-hello.service
[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
EOF
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now lab-hello.timer
systemctl list-timers --no-pager | head
steps.explain: Timer units are the systemd-native replacement for cron, with journal integration.

steps.title: Mount unit example
steps.code:
mkdir -p /mnt/labdata
cat > /etc/systemd/system/mnt-labdata.mount <<'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Tmpfs at /mnt/labdata
[Mount]
What=tmpfs
Where=/mnt/labdata
Type=tmpfs
Options=size=16M
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl start mnt-labdata.mount
mount | grep labdata
steps.explain: Mount unit filenames must match the path with / replaced by - (/mnt/labdata -> mnt-labdata.mount).

steps.title: Boot performance
steps.code:
systemd-analyze
systemd-analyze blame | head
systemd-analyze critical-chain | head -20
steps.explain: blame ranks units by start time; critical-chain traces the longest dependency delay path.

steps.title: Host, time, and DNS
steps.code:
hostnamectl
hostnamectl set-hostname linuxplus-lab
timedatectl
timedatectl set-timezone Asia/Singapore
resolvectl status | head -20
steps.explain: These systemd front ends manage /etc/hostname, the clock/timezone, and the stub DNS resolver.

test: systemctl list-timers shows lab-hello.timer scheduled and journalctl -u lab-hello records each firing, confirming the custom units work.

title: Containers with Docker & Podman
obj_title: Manage applications in a container on a Linux server
goal: Install Docker and Podman, pull/run images, manage volumes and networks, build an image from a Dockerfile, and contrast rootful vs rootless engines.
build: Run and inspect nginx containers, bind-mount volumes, build an image, wire a user-defined network, and try rootless Podman.

steps.title: Install Docker and Podman
steps.code:
apt update -y
apt install -y docker.io podman
systemctl start docker
docker --version
podman --version
steps.explain: docker.io is Ubuntu's Docker Engine; podman is a daemonless, rootless-friendly alternative.

steps.title: Pull and run a container
steps.code:
docker pull nginx
docker images
docker run -d -p 8080:80 --name web nginx
docker ps
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:8080
steps.explain: -d detaches, -p publishes a port, --name assigns a friendly identifier.

steps.title: Inspect, exec, logs
steps.code:
docker logs web | head
docker exec -it web bash -c "nginx -v; ls /usr/share/nginx/html"
docker inspect web | head -40
docker stats --no-stream web
steps.explain: exec runs a new process inside a running container; inspect returns its full JSON config.

steps.title: Volumes for persistent data
steps.code:
mkdir -p /data echo "

Lab 13

" > /data/index.html docker run -d -p 8081:80 -v /data:/usr/share/nginx/html:ro --name web2 nginx curl -s http://localhost:8081
steps.explain: Bind mounts expose host paths in the container; :ro makes the mount read-only.

steps.title: Build a tiny image
steps.code:
mkdir -p /tmp/hello && cd /tmp/hello
cat > app.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/sh
echo "Hello from $(hostname) as $(id -un)"
EOF
chmod +x app.sh
cat > Dockerfile <<'EOF'
FROM alpine:3.20
RUN adduser -D appuser
COPY app.sh /usr/local/bin/app.sh
USER appuser
ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/app.sh"]
CMD []
EOF
docker build -t hello:1 .
docker run --rm hello:1
steps.explain: FROM sets the base, USER drops root, ENTRYPOINT is the fixed command, CMD its default args.

steps.title: Networks and port mapping
steps.code:
docker network create labnet
docker run -d --network labnet --name api nginx
docker run --rm --network labnet alpine sh -c "apk add --no-cache curl >/dev/null && curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://api"
docker network inspect labnet | head -30
steps.explain: User-defined bridge networks provide built-in DNS so containers reach each other by name.

steps.title: Rootless Podman and privileged note
steps.code:
podman run --rm alpine echo "hello from podman"
podman ps -a
echo "--privileged disables most isolation: device access, capabilities, AppArmor."
echo "Prefer unprivileged + specific --cap-add over --privileged."
steps.explain: Podman runs daemonless and, as a non-root user, in a rootless user namespace for defense in depth.

test: curl against the published ports returns 200 and the alpine container reaches http://api by name, confirming containers, volumes, and the user-defined network work.
